blocks.text: Steven Lowe
VP Finance
Key Group
Re: Designing a shared services organization
Dear Mr. Lowe:

We are glad to take on this work, and this letter puts our understanding in writing before it starts. It sets out the scope, the fee, the schedule, and the terms that govern the engagement between our firm and {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}. I have written it in plain terms and kept it short. Read it against what we discussed; if anything reads differently, call me before you sign and I will correct the wording.
The Work

Key Group today runs finance, human resources, information technology, and procurement support inside each of its operating units. The same roles sit in unit after unit, and no one owns the question of whether that is the right way to deliver them. You have asked us to answer it: whether those functions are better provided from a single shared organization, and, where they are, to design that organization and the route to standing it up. This is a design engagement. It ends with a recommended model and an implementation plan, not with the model in operation.
The work proceeds along four lines.

items: A baseline of the support functions as they run today: what each unit spends on them, how many people perform them, and the service levels they deliver.

A design for the processes a shared organization would take on, together with its service catalogue, the service levels attached to each service, and the way its costs are charged back to the units it serves.
An organization and governance design: reporting lines, spans of control, where the work sits, and the decision rights that keep the shared organization answerable to the units.
A business case and a staged plan to stand the organization up, sequenced so that the least disruptive moves come first and each stage stands on its own.

blocks.text: It is worth marking the edges of the work, so that nothing you expect is left unstated. We design the shared organization; we do not operate it, hire into it, or run the transition once it begins. We do not give legal, tax, or accounting advice; where a question turns on any of those, we identify it and refer you to your own advisers. We do not select or install software; where the analysis reaches a systems question, we frame it and leave the decision with you.
Our Approach

We build from Key Group's own operating and financial records rather than from sector benchmarks, and every figure in a deliverable traces to a workpaper we can produce on request. We keep findings apart from recommendations and label each, because the two do not carry equal weight. We bring interim results to you as they firm up, so that the final material contains nothing you are seeing for the first time. Where a number rests on data we do not yet hold in full, we state it as a range and say so, and we close the range before it carries a recommendation.
Who Will Do the Work

Jason Torres, Principal, leads the engagement and is your point of contact on anything of substance in the work. Daniel Cisneros, Research Associate, assembles the baseline data and the cost model and keeps the record of what we were given and by whom. I read each deliverable before it leaves this office. On your side, we will treat you, Steven Lowe, as our contact for scheduling, data, and the decisions the work calls for, unless you name someone else to hold that role.
Fee and Timing

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} is {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope described above. We expect to begin on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}} and to reach a final recommendation and implementation plan approximately eight months later. If you ask us to widen the scope, we will price the addition in writing and will not begin it until you have agreed that price. We prefer a fixed fee here so that your people can work with us without watching a clock.
Billing and Payment

We invoice the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Out-of-pocket costs, chiefly travel to your locations, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. We do not mark them up.
Duration and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date above until we deliver the final recommendation and plan, unless one of us ends it earlier. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice, for any reason or none. On termination you owe the fee for the work performed to the effective date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses already incurred, and we hand over the work in whatever state it has then reached.
Confidentiality

The cost, headcount, and service data behind an operating-model decision is among the most sensitive information a company holds, and we treat it so. We will use what you give us only for this engagement, and disclose it outside our firm only as you direct in writing or as the law requires. We ask the same of you with respect to our methods, our workpapers, and the fee recorded in this letter.
Limits on Liability

Our total liability arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract, in tort, or on any other basis, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, however arising. This limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you intend to put one before a third party who may rely on it, raise it with me first so that we can address the reliance in writing.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute we cannot settle between us will go to mediation before either party turns to a court.

To proceed, please countersign both originals and return one to me for our file; the other is yours to keep. We are ready to begin this work for Key Group on the date named above, and Jason Torres will be in touch to arrange the first working session.
Sincerely,
To: Jason Torres
From: Daniel Cisneros
Subject: Kickoff, shared services design for Key Group

We begin the shared services design for Key Group on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}. This memo sets the objective and the division of work so the two of us start from the same definitions. The question {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} has put to us is narrow: whether finance, human resources, information technology, and procurement support are better run from one shared organization than from inside each unit, and if so, how that organization should be built and stood up. Everything below serves that question. Steven Lowe, their VP Finance, is our sponsor and the source of the data and the access we will need.

Two standing rules on the client side, so we do not have to relitigate them later. Requests to Steven Lowe's staff go out consolidated and logged, so that no one is asked twice for the same file. Unit-level cost and headcount data is confidential; it stays inside the team and does not travel.
How the work divides

items: Baseline. I assemble current spend, headcount, and service levels for the four functions across every unit, and I record where each figure came from. When the client questions a number, I want to show its source rather than rebuild it.

Service design. Jason Torres defines which processes a shared organization would take on, the service catalogue, and the service levels attached to each. This is where the scope of the shared organization is actually set.

Organization and cost model. I build the cost model beneath each design option; Jason Torres fixes the reporting lines, the spans, and the location. We hold the process and cost definitions steady, so two of us are never counting a function differently.
Business case and roadmap. Last, and resting on the first three. Jason Torres drafts the staged plan and the case for it; I test the numbers underneath before anything reaches the client.

blocks.text: The dependency worth naming now is that the design cannot outrun the baseline. Service levels and chargeback rules mean nothing until the current cost and headcount are measured per function per unit, so the early weeks weigh toward data, and the design firms up only as those figures close.
The first two weeks

items: I send one consolidated data request this week and open the request log, so nothing is asked of Steven Lowe's staff twice.
Jason Torres fixes the definitions for a process, a full-time equivalent, and a service level before any figure is calculated, so we are not renegotiating what a number means in month four.
We schedule interviews with the function leads in each unit and draft the question set for review beforehand.
The two of us meet at the end of next week to compare what the data shows so far, gaps included.

blocks.text: There is nothing here the client would read as a finding, because we have none: we have a scope, a plan, and a data request about to go out the door. I will keep the log current so we always know where each request stands for Key Group. If anything above looks wrong to you, tell me before the request goes out and I will adjust it.

To: Steven Lowe
Cc: Jason Torres
Steven,

Thank you for getting the first round of spend and headcount figures across so quickly. I have the finance and procurement numbers loaded and reconciled against the ledgers you sent, and the human resources and IT figures are close behind; I expect both to be reconciled by the end of next week. We are not drawing conclusions yet, and will not until the baseline is complete for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}.

Two things would help us keep pace. We are still short the current service levels for the IT help desk and the payroll cycle, which we need before any comparison across units means much; if those live in a system rather than a document, a read-only extract is fine and we will pull the detail ourselves. And Jason Torres would like to sit with the function leads in your two largest units over the next couple of weeks; could you point us to the right person to arrange the times? An hour with each is enough to start.

Everything the Key Group team has sent so far has come in clean and well labeled, which makes the work faster. I will copy you as items on the request log close; nothing on it needs a decision from you this week beyond the two asks above.
Thanks,
Daniel Cisneros
Working session on the shared services design for Key Group, held at the client's offices. Minutes taken by Jason Torres.
Attendees

items: Jason Torres, Principal
Daniel Cisneros, Research Associate
Steven Lowe, VP Finance, {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}

blocks.text: Discussion

We are far enough into the baseline to put real numbers against the four functions, and the session was about what those numbers show and where the design goes from here. Nothing today is a recommendation, and I said so at the start.

Daniel Cisneros walked the baseline. Spend and headcount are in and reconciled for finance, procurement, and human resources across every unit; IT is complete but for one unit's help-desk figures, which are still being normalized. He kept what is confirmed apart from what is still being cleaned, and nothing we discussed today rests on the one open item.

The pattern is plain. Three of the four functions run at a scale inside each unit that a shared organization would not need: the same roles repeat unit by unit, and the cost per transaction sits well above what a consolidated team would carry. Procurement is the clearest case, finance close behind. Human resources is more mixed, because a share of its work is local by nature and does not move to a shared team. My read is that the design should consolidate transactional finance and procurement in the first wave and treat HR as a partial move, and I want the option memo built that way.

A few numbers are worth recording. Finance and procurement together carry more supervisory layers than their volume warrants; the span of control in those teams is narrow, and a good deal of the cost sits in the second and third layers rather than in the people doing the work. Daniel Cisneros also flagged one figure that still carries a qualification. The procurement spend for the two units that share a vendor master is stated within a range until their records reconcile against a single source, and the range affects the size of the read, not its direction.

Steven Lowe's main question was whether the units keep enough control over their service once the work moves. That is a governance question rather than a cost one, and it belongs in the design. We agreed the service catalogue and the chargeback rules have to answer it before any number goes to his leadership. He also asked us to hold the smallest unit out of the first wave, on the ground that its volumes are too low to move the case; I agreed, and we will document it that way.

We closed on what the next session has to show. The option memo will set the first-wave scope, the service catalogue, and a first cut of the chargeback rules, and it will keep the smallest unit visibly outside the first wave so that no one has to ask. Steven Lowe asked that the memo state, for each function that moves, what a unit keeps and what it hands over, because that distinction is what his own managers will read first.
Action items

rows: Close the open IT help-desk figures and finish the baseline
Draft the service catalogue and chargeback rules that answer the units' control concern
Build the first design option: transactional finance and procurement in the first wave, HR partial
Confirm which units fall in the first wave and which are held back
Set the next working session once the option memo is drafted

blocks.text: Status Report: Shared Services Design

Prepared by Daniel Cisneros for the finance sponsor at Key Group, covering the four weeks to this report and reviewed by Jason Torres. Client of record: {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}. The engagement stands at roughly its midpoint.
Where the work stands

The engagement reached its midpoint this period and is on plan. The baseline of the four support functions is complete, and we have begun turning it into the first design option for the shared organization. Measured against the engagement budget of {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}, we are a little under half spent, which tracks the work done rather than the calendar; the heavier design and business-case hours fall in the periods ahead, and the budget was built around that. Nothing found so far points to a change in the fee or the schedule.

Put simply: the measurement phase is essentially done, the numbers match what we expected at the outset, and the work now moves from describing how the four functions run today to designing how a shared organization would run them. We are not recommending a design yet, and will not until the options are built and tested.
Completed this period

items: Finished the baseline of spend, headcount, and service levels for finance, procurement, human resources, and IT across every operating unit.
Reconciled the function-level costs to the general ledger with your finance team, so the baseline matches the figures your own reporting produces.
Confirmed that the cost per transaction in finance and procurement runs well above what a consolidated team would carry, which is the result that points toward a shared model.
Closed the last open data item, the IT help-desk figures for one unit, so there are no outstanding requests on our side.

blocks.text: We have kept all of this on the measurement side of the line. Each result feeds the design, and we mark it that way on every exhibit, so that a figure is not read as a conclusion before one exists. The one point already firm enough to state is direction, not magnitude: the cost of running these functions inside each unit is higher than a shared model would carry, and the coming period is about sizing that gap and testing what it would take to close it.
The coming period

items: Jason Torres develops the first design option: which processes the shared organization takes on, its service catalogue, and the service levels attached to each.
We draft the chargeback rules that keep the shared organization answerable to the units it serves, since control, not cost, is the units' first concern.
We build the cost model beneath the design, so the business case rests on the same figures as the baseline.

We bring a first, clearly labeled design option to the next working session, its cost and its effect on service set out side by side, so the session weighs a real option instead of reacting to a finished answer.

blocks.text: Risks we are watching

items: Unit control. The unit heads worry about losing control of their service once work moves to a shared team. This is a governance question, and if the catalogue and chargeback rules do not answer it convincingly the design will stall whatever the saving. We are treating it as a design requirement, not an afterthought.
The smallest unit. Its volumes are low enough that folding it into the first wave would add cost and risk for little saving. We expect to hold it out, and will say so plainly in the option memo.

Scope discipline. Two of your managers have raised systems questions that sit just outside the design scope. We have noted them and will flag any that would change the fee before doing the work, not after.

blocks.text: In short, the engagement is where it should be at the halfway mark, and the coming period turns the baseline into a design your leadership can weigh. If the next period runs to plan, the first option will be in your hands well before the final month. I will keep this report on its monthly cadence for Key Group, and the request log stays current for anyone on your side who wants to see what is outstanding.
Notes from the working session on the shared services design for Key Group, at the client's offices. Recorded by Jason Torres.
Present at the session

items: Jason Torres, Principal
Daniel Cisneros, Research Associate
Steven Lowe, VP Finance, Key Group

blocks.text: Discussion

This session was about the transition, not the design. The model is settled: transactional finance and procurement consolidate in the first wave, the transactional part of human resources moves a step behind, and the smallest unit stays out until the second year. What remained was to agree the sequence and the terms on which we recommend it.

Daniel Cisneros presented the business case. The one-time cost to stand the shared organization up and the annual saving once it runs are both firm now, and every figure traces to a workpaper. He noted that the saving holds even on the conservative volume assumption, which matters, because the case should not lean on a strong year to look good.

Two figures in the case are worth recording. The one-time cost is weighted toward the first wave, because the finance and procurement move carries the systems and relocation work while the later HR step is lighter. And the payback, on the conservative assumption, still falls inside the second year, which is the test Steven Lowe set at the outset for whether the move earns the disruption it causes.

My recommendation is the staged plan we have costed: finance and procurement first, over the back half of the year; the transactional HR work a quarter behind them; the smallest unit deferred. I would not compress it further. The saving is real, but the risk in this kind of move sits in the cutover, and a first wave that moves at a deliberate pace is what protects the service levels the units are worried about.

On the transition itself, Daniel Cisneros and I walked the cutover for the first wave. The controls that matter are the ones that keep the month-end close intact while the finance work moves, and the plan holds the old and new teams in parallel for one full close before the handover completes. It costs a few weeks of double running, and it is the cost I am least willing to cut, because a missed close is the one failure the units would not forgive.

Steven Lowe pressed on two points before he takes this to his leadership. First, he wants the chargeback model shown as it would appear on a unit's own budget, so each unit head sees exactly what changes for them. Second, he wants the service levels in the catalogue stated as commitments the shared organization is held to, not as aspirations. Both are fair, and both belong in the final pack. We agreed the final recommendation to {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} leadership will carry the staged plan, the business case, and the governance terms as a single document.
Owners and next actions

items: Daniel Cisneros: restate the chargeback model at the unit level and confirm every figure ties to a workpaper. Before the final review.
Jason Torres: finalize the staged transition plan and the service-level commitments in the catalogue. Before the final review.
Jason Torres: assemble the final recommendation as one pack, with the plan, the case, and the governance terms together. Before the final review.
Steven Lowe: schedule the leadership review and confirm who from each unit should see the chargeback detail. Within two weeks.
